docs.description: Ars Rediviva, founded by Claude Crussard - BAM records 1936 - 1945 Discographical data Ars Rediviva, Boîte à Musique records 1936-1945 01   Johann Philipp Krieger: Sonata in E mineur       Edmée Ormans-Bach & Dominique Blot, violin       Yvonne Thibout, cello       Claude Crussard, piano       78 rpm 30 cm:  BAM 5       Matrix nrs.:  77641 / 77623       Recorded:  Paris, 1936 01 20       Johann Sebastian Bach: Sonata in G major BWV 1039 02  1  adagio 03   2  allegro ma non troppo 04  3  adagio e piano 05  4  presto       René le Roy & André Musset, flute       Claude Crussard, harpsichord       78 rpm 25 cm:  BAM 11-12       Matrix nrs.:  PART 954-957       Recorded:  Paris, november 1937 06  Georg Friedrich Händel: Sonata in G minor op. 2        andante - allegro - arioso - allegro       Dominique Blot & Edmée Ortmans-Bach, violin;        Yvonne Thiboud, cello;        Claude Crussard, harpsichord       78 rpm 30 cm:  BAM 24       Matrix nrs.:  PART 1437-2 / 1438-2       Recorded:  Paris, march 1939       attributed to Johann Gottlieb Goldberg: Sonata in C major (BWV 1060) 07  1  adagio 08  2  fugue 09  3  largo 10  4  gigue       Edmée Ortmans-Bach & Dominique Blot, violin       Jacqueline Alliaume, cello       Claude Crussard, harpsichord       78 rpm 30 cm:  BAM 28-29       Matrix nrs.:  PARTX 2199-1; 2200-1; 2201-1; 2202-1       Recorded:  Paris, march 1945 Claude Crussard (31.08.1893 – 01.02.1947), full name Germaine Louise Micheline Crussard, was a French musicologist, harpsichordist and pianist who played an important role in the revival of seventeenth- and eighteenth-century music in France. After entering the Paris Conservatoire in 1915, she was awarded a Premier Prix in fugue in 1924. During the following decade she increasingly devoted herself to the study, editing and performance of early music. Crussard's work formed part of a broader movement that emerged during the interwar years. In France, musicians and scholars such as Wanda Landowska, Henri Casadesus ( Société des Instruments Anciens )  and Curt Sachs ( "2000 years of music" - Parlophone; label L'Anthologie Sonore since 1933 - mid 1950s) were helping to renew interest in music of earlier centuries. Sachs, after settling in Paris in 1934, launched the influential recording series L'Anthologie Sonore , which sought to present medieval, Renaissance and Baroque music in performances based on contemporary musicological research. Within this environment, the rediscovery of forgotten repertoire became an important feature of French musical life. In 1935 Claude Crussard founded the ensemble Ars Rediviva , which specialized in music of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. The ensemble's personnel varied according to the repertoire and included such musicians as Edmée Ortmans-Bach, Dominique Blot (18.11.1912 - 1.2.1947), Sonia Lovis (real name Lhuissier; 1925 - 1.2.1947)), Yvonne Thibout, Jacqueline Alliaume, together with a number of guest instrumentalists and singers like René le Roy, André Musset and Fernand Caratgé. Ars Rediviva performed works by Bach, Handel, Vivaldi, Scarlatti and Rameau, while also reviving music by composers then seldom heard in concert, among them Marc-Antoine Charpentier, Leclair, Telemann, Schmelzer, Dornel, Francœur and Blavet. Crussard was particularly active in researching neglected French Baroque music, preparing performing materials from archival sources and helping to bring forgotten works before the public. In 1945 she published Un musicien français oublié, Marc-Antoine Charpentier , 1634–1704 . Claude Crussard's final concerts took place in January 1947. On 1 February 1947, while travelling to Portugal for a series of performances, the aircraft carrying Crussard and several members of Ars Rediviva crashed near Sintra. Among those killed were Claude Crussard, Edmée Ortmans-Bach, Dominique Blot, Jacqueline Alliaume-Pariot, Bernard Coqueret and Monique Coqueret-Brothier, Sonia Lovis, Monique Fournery-Deshays and Claire Crussard, the ensemble's secretary. Fournery-Deshays was replacing the ensemble's regular flautist, Fernand Caratgé, who therefore escaped the disaster. The crash brought an abrupt end to the activities of Ars Rediviva. According to several later sources, the name Ars Rediviva was adopted in 1951 by a newly founded Prague ensemble, on the suggestion of Jean-Pierre Rampal, who encouraged  to revive the name of Claude Crussard's French Ars Rediviva .  The following preliminary discography has been compiled from surviving records, label scans, a contemporary BAM catalogue, the CHARM database, Michael Gray's A Classical Discography, and Alan Kelly's matrix studies. Dates and attributions remain subject to revision as additional evidence becomes available. docs.creator: Johann Sebastian Bach; Glenn Gould
docs.description: "Gould Bach" was a brand name while the Canadian pianist was alive -- and it remains a brand name in this 2007 Sony-BMG CD reissue. Recorded in 1971 and first released by Columbia on LP in 1974 as Vol. 2 of Gould's survey of Bach's French Suites plus the Overture in the French Style, the performances here were considered highly controversial in their time. Gould's fans hailed his flawless technique and intellectuality. But his detractors, while not dismissing his virtuosity, disdained what they called his quirky eccentricities and, of course, his irrepressible humming that was always audible on the recordings. But Gould's fans also included portions of the larger listening audience and the consensus ultimately came down on the side of Gould as the preeminent rugged individualist among Bach players. This 2007 CD reissue duplicates the program and the cover art as well as the translucent stereo sound of the LP original. Through it, listeners unfamiliar with Gould can hear his dry Allemandes, his crisp Gavottes, his détaché Sarabandes, his buoyant Gigues, and his relentless vocalizing and decide for themselves where they stand. docs.creator: Bach–Avakyan
docs.description: This is a demonstration of the Avak Avakyan’s harmonic system «Nonaphony» (resume: the minor second, the tritone and the augmented triad is absent in the harmonic vertical). J.S. Bach — Well–Tempered Clavier, Book 1 Prelude 24 in B minor, BWV 869 — with «Nonaphony» amendments, created by Avak Avakyan. Avak Avakyan’s idea: the eroticism in music is contained in the minor second in the harmonic vertical. The tritone and the augmented triad contain the small second in loud overtones. The goal of nonaphony is music without eroticism. The music score (pdf) You can see  here ( click )  .  The video (avi) You can see  here ( click )  .

docs.creator: Johann Sebastian Bach; Sviatoslav Richter
docs.description: Acclaimed as one of the most creative and thoughtful performers of J.S. Bach's keyboard music since the innovative performances of her compatriot, Glenn Gould, Canadian pianist Angela Hewitt has certainly matched her usual exquisite playing in this 2008 set of The Well-Tempered Clavier, an anticipated follow-up to the 2007 reissue of her 1997 recordings. Having both sets would be ideal for Hewitt fans, but for listeners who can choose only one, either is an excellent option and sure not to disappoint. While no one should expect vast differences in her interpretations here, which are rich in the variety of tone colors, moods, and nuances, Hewitt's maturing appreciation of Bach is not a radical overhaul, though there are necessarily changes in the particulars of accentuation, phrasing, dynamics, and emphasis due to the passage of time, as well as to the spontaneity of Hewitt's expression and the fluidity of the moment. With a subtle shaping of phrases and a clean sense of line, Hewitt brings across the 48 preludes and fugues with assured technique and generous expression, and sufficient differences of approach to complement her earlier set, but some of her tempos and adjustments seem a trifle exaggerated in these performances, where the earlier recordings seem a bit tighter, neater, and sometimes more focused. Recorded in the Jesus-Christus-Kirche, Berlin, following her world tour of 58 cities in 21 countries, Hewitt's performances are presented on four discs in this trimline box set from Hyperion, and the sound quality is only slightly less vibrant than her first go-round with The Well-Tempered Clavier. Highly recommended. &copy TiVo \

docs.creator: Francesco D'Avalos, The Philharmonia Orchestra, Judith Howarth, Jean Rigby, Bach Choir, Kenneth Smith, John Anderson, Michael Collins, Meyrick Alexander and Richard Watkins
docs.description: Felix Mendelssohn (1809-1847) A Midsummer Night's Dream, Op. 21 and Op. 61 Overture and 7 Excerpts ( suite) Orchestra: Philharmonia Orchestra  Cond.: Francesco D'Avalos (1930-2014) Fairy 1: Judith Howarth  (Song and Finale) Fairy 2: Jean Rigby  (Song) Fairy Chorus: Bach Choir, Women  (Song and Finale) Flute (solo): Kenneth Smith (Scherzo) Oboe: John Anderson  (Scherzo) Clarinet: Michael Collins  (Scherzo) Bassoon: Meyrick Alexander  (Scherzo) Horn (solo): Richard Watkins (Notturno) Libretto: William Shakespeare  (Song and Finale) Studio Recording Date: 1993. June 23  Studio Recording Place: Watford Town Hall, England, UK IMHO, this is truly the BEST performance!!! [0:00] Overture (Op. 21) [10:45] Scherzo (Op. 61) [15:12] "Ye spotted snakes" (Op. 61)  Eng. ver. [19:27] Intermezzo (Op. 61) [22:35] Notturno (Op. 61) [28:27] "Wedding March" (Op. 61) [32:38] Bergamesk Dance (Op. 61) [34:06] Finale ("Tro' the house")  (Op. 61)  Eng. ver. Even though Mendelssohn, a German composer, created the music to fit a German translation of Shakespeare's play and the first production was in German in 1843, the work is more likely to be performed frequently and internationally with the ORIGINAL  Shakespearean E nglish libretto , which is the actual language of the play, in which this recording is heard, rather than the German (which is still performed besides the English version). The American 2002 TELARC release uses the same recording as the UK 1994 original release, but edited and modified, along with the addition of spoken extracts from Shakespeare'a comedy. The second through fourth melodramas and the music for the rustic players were omitted, except for the first melodrama and the "Fairies March". The "Wedding March" is also edited out to be shortened and transitioned into its reprise and the recall of the fairy music from the overture's opening. The American release also used 7 all-star actors who were famous for being on screen with live-action and animated roles — these were Ben Kingsley (Oberon), Julie Andrews (Titania), Danny DeVito (Puck), Hynden Walch (Fairy), Ken Mars (Duke of Athens), Jodi Benson (Hermia) and Christopher Daniel Barnes (Lysander). The spoken extracts were recorded in Hollywood in 1995, while the music is still recorded in 1993, with kind permission and courtesy of IMP. However, this recording with spoken text got shelved into its drawers. Programme notes: Inspired by William Shakespeare's comedy A Midsummer Night's Dream, Felix Mendelssohn completed his famous eponymous overture, Op. 21 in 1826, and the remaining music in 1841. A Midsummer Night's Dream had always been a favourite of Felix and his sister, Fanny. The overture was by an incredibly musically gifted youth of 17. The commission for the remaining music came from Prussia's King Friedrich Wilhelm IV's Academy of the Arts and the Leipzig Gewandhaus Orchestra, for a Potsdam production of the play, one of several commissions for theatrical music Mendelssohn received while in this post. The producer of the play was Ludwig Tieck, one of the translators of the definitive German version of the play, the same version that the Mendelssohns had enjoyed and absorbed thoroughly as their own.  The incidental music consists of 14 sections, including the overture. There are vocal selections and instrumental movements. The vocal selections include the song "Ye spotted snakes” , the Finale’s "Tro' the house" and melodramas "Over hill, over dale” , "The Spells” , "What hempen homespuns"  and "The Removal of the Spells” . The melodramas served to enhance Shakespeare's text. (These are not included in this recording.) The remaining sections are primarily cues. The music combines the traditional forms and structures of Classical music with the feeling and expression of the Romantic era. Throughout the sections, Mendelssohn sprinkles themes and motives pulled from the earlier overture to create coherence. The overture calls for pairs of woodwinds, horns and trumpets, tuba, tympani and strings. The incidental music adds a third trumpet, three trombones, triangle, cymbals and a soprano, a mezzo and a women's chorus for the vocal selections.  The overture is full of brilliant touches. The four woodwind chords represent the words "Once upon a time". The fairy-world comes alive with scurrying high string passages while grand outbursts by the orchestra picture the Duke of Athens' hunting party. A tender string melody pictures the romance of the couples: Hermia with Lysander and Helena with Demetrius. The long brays in the bassoon accompany Bottom’s transformation into a donkey. The feather-light Scherzo appropriately introduces the fairy-world of Act Two with rapid, running passages in the woodwinds, notably the flutes and clarinets, similar to the string passage in the opening of the overture, both set in a minor mode. The rest of the orchestra joins the woodwinds in a Classical sonata-form movement. Several small motives are repeated, up and down, then down and up the scale, to form the development section. The recapitulation section rounds off with a virtuosic flute melody. The lyrical "Ye spotted snakes"  is cast in two couplets. A string bass line and murmuring of the woodwinds suggests the buzzing of the creatures, as Titania's fairy chorus bids her good-night. The Intermezzo represents the confusion encountered as Hermia awakes, with a swirling melody buffeted about by the orchestra. The rustic players enter jauntily, represented by the bassoons and ending the Intermezzo in the major. A German Romantic horn melody is the theme of the Notturno. The music evokes the dreams of the couples as Puck puts right his previous mischief. The "Wedding March" opens with that oh-so-familiar trumpet fanfare, fitting for the Duke of Athens' wedding. Two trio sections are separated by the opening theme; the final occurrence of the main theme includes twittering flutes and strings, suggesting the fairies' part in the matchmaking. Triumphant chords in the brass and strings close the "Wedding March". The robust “Bergamesk Dance” for the rustic players recalls the donkey’s braying from the secondary theme of the overture, and the "Finale" returns to the overture for most of its sparkling material,  ending with the same four woodwind chords that begin the entire work. The magic is over. Although some consider Mendelssohn's work to be lightweight and uninspired, the entirety of A Midsummer Night's Dream proves otherwise in its inventiveness in reviving his older material and in its expression. When hearing the overture and the incidental music altogether, this is music of which Shakespeare would surely have approved. - © 2002 Patsy Morita (TiVo) Libretto    Both sung in the ORIGINAL Shakespearean English version. Ye spotted snakes Ye spotted snakes with double tongue, Thorny hedgehogs, be not seen Newts and blindworms, do no wrong, Come not near our Fairy Queen Hence, away! Philomel, with melody Sing in our sweet lullaby Lulla, lulla, lullaby, lulla, lulla, lullaby Never harm Nor spell nor charm Come our lovely lady nigh So good night, with lullaby Weaving spiders, come not here Hence, you long-legged spinners, hence Beetles black, approach not near Worm nor snail, do no offence Come not here Philomel… etc. Hence, away! Now all is well One aloof stand sentinel Tro' the house Tro' the house give glim'ring light, By the dead and drowsy fire Every elf and fairy sprite, Hop as light as bird from brier, And this ditty after me, Sing and dance it trippingly First rehearse your song by rote, To each word a warbling note Hand in hand, with fairy grace, Will we sing and bless this place Trip away  Make no stay Meet him all by break of day I DO NOT make money off of any of my videos and these ARE NOT my songs. Copyright notices are below this note because I don't want them to act as tags for search results. I want this video to be seen strictly by record and CD enthusiasts and not music fans in general. I'd much prefer to make original videos on my main channel if only I wasn't so horribly uncreative.  This video uses content from the Philharmonia and Francesco D'Avalos' "Complete Incidental Music to A Midsummer Night's Dream, Op 61 and The Fair Melusina Overture, Op. 32". docs.description: Johann Sebastian Bach (1685-1750) Passacaglia in c minor BWV 582 - Thema fugatum (billed on disc labels as ‘Fugue en ut mineur’) Madame Édouard Gautier(?) (pedal harmonium) Recorded : c. 1947/48, residence of Madame Édouard Gautier, Paris Format : coarse-groove, 78 rpm, 10” / 25 cm, electrical Matrices : ST 2599 / ST 2600 Catalogue number : 719 Mobile recording by Studio Technisonor, Paris Recording date extrapolated from highest Technisonor matrix known to late discographer Gérard Roig (1934-2011) Madame Édouard Gautier was dedicatee of Marcel Dupré’s Élévation in G Op.32 No.3, published 1935 On 13 December 1936, Madame Gautier gave a public recital on the organ of the Église de la Sainte-Trinité in Paris, performing works by J.S. Bach, Olivier Messiaen (then titular organist of la Sainte-Trinité) and Marcel Dupré In 1941 she gave eight recitals at the Église Saint-Séverin The same Madame Édouard Gautier is believed to be the owner of the instrument heard on this disc (and at least two others, of music by Messiaen, not available for transfer) She has been identified as Marie-Thérèse Gautier, née María Teresa Angulo y Sánchez de Movellán (1882-1959), and was very probably the performer recorded on this disc, although this cannot be confirmed; the location was therefore the Gautiers’ residence at 7, av. Franklin D. Roosevelt, Paris 8e Many thanks to M. Bruno Chaumet (President, Association des Amis de l’Art de Marcel Dupré ) and, especially, M. Denis Havard de la Montagne (Editor-in-Chief, Musica et Memoria ) for their kind help Transfer and restoration : Andrew Hallifax for DogintheManger Editing and photographs : DogintheManger Contemporary sources No notices located
